Title: Regulation of Stomatal Tropism and Infection by Light in Cercospora zeae-maydis: Evidence for Coordinated Host/Pathogen Responses to Photoperiod?

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: strain SCOH1-5; Dphl1-1 and Dcrp1Dphl1 backgrounds mentioned
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: induced during photoreactivation in wild type
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: induced by light after UV exposure; no induction in crp1 mutant
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: UV irradiation followed by white light; constant light for cercosporin assays
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: previous functional characterization cited; double mutant analysis with CRP1; qRT-PCR during photoreactivation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required for photoreactivation but not a major determinant of cercosporin accumulation timing in this study
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PHL1 encodes a photolyase previously shown to function in light-dependent DNA repair. 2. In this study, PHL1 expression was strongly induced during photoreactivation in the wild type but not in the crp1 mutant. 3. A crp1 phl1 double mutant still accumulated wild-type levels of cercosporin after 7 days, indicating PHL1 has little role in cercosporin induction.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it was experimentally tested in the Results, but it is not the primary nutrient-metabolism gene and does not directly define cercosporin biosynthesis regulation.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: induced during photoreactivation in wild type
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: weakly induced in crp1 and crp1 phl1 mutants relative to wild type after UV/light treatment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: UV irradiation followed by white light photoreactivation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR during photoreactivation in wild type, crp1 mutant, and crp1 phl1 double mutant
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CRP1-dependent induction during photoreactivation; no direct nutrient metabolite role shown
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPD1 is predicted to encode a CPD photolyase. 2. Its expression was strongly induced during photoreactivation in the wild type. 3. Induction was much lower in crp1 mutants, indicating CRP1 regulates CPD1 expression during UV damage repair.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because experimentally analyzed in Results, but not a nutrient metabolism core gene.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: fungal strain SCOH1-5; maize inbred B73
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: photosensitizing phytotoxin contributing to foliar necrosis and pathogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: culture medium; infected maize leaf tissue during pathogenesis inferred
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: White Collar-1-like photoreceptor; LOV domain; PAS-fold domain; PAS domain; GATA zinc-finger DNA-binding domain; nuclear localization signal
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: blue-light perception coupled to transcriptional regulation; activates light-responsive genes and represses conidiation/cercosporin in non-inductive conditions
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: secondary metabolite biosynthesis (cercosporin), photoreactivation-associated DNA repair gene induction, and developmental regulation linked to infection
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: induces PHL1 and CPD1 during photoreactivation; promotes timely light-induced cercosporin biosynthesis; represses cercosporin biosynthesis in red light/darkness; represses conidiation in white/blue light
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: PHL1; CPD1; cercosporin biosynthetic process (specific structural genes not identified)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Putative White Collar Complex-like function inferred from homology to WC-1; interacting partner not experimentally validated in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: blue light; white light; UV exposure during photoreactivation; photoperiod
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: constant darkness; 12 h light/dark cycle; constant white light; blue light (400–530 nm); red light (600–700 nm); UV irradiation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: targeted gene disruption by homologous recombination; pathogenicity assays on maize; scanning electron microscopy; metabolite profiling of cercosporin; conidiation assays; UV survival/photoreactivation assays; qRT-PCR of PHL1 and CPD1
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: required for stomatal tropism, efficient appressorium formation, foliar necrosis, repression of conidiation in light, timely induction of cercosporin biosynthesis, and photoreactivation after UV
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRP1 encodes a WC-1-like blue-light photoreceptor/transcription factor in C. zeae-maydis. 2. Disruption of CRP1 abolished stomatal tropism and reduced appressorium formation about 10-fold, preventing lesion development on maize. 3. CRP1 mediated blue-light repression of conidiation. 4. Loss of CRP1 delayed, but did not eliminate, cercosporin accumulation in white/blue light and derepressed cercosporin production in red light and darkness. 5. CRP1 was essential for photoreactivation and for strong induction of PHL1 and CPD1 after UV exposure.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: major light-responsive regulator controlling cercosporin biosynthesis timing and conditional repression, while also coordinating infection-related development
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CRP1 disruption causes severe loss of pathogenicity and altered sporulation; possible pleiotropic effects on multiple light responses
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: targeting CRP1 or its signaling pathway could reduce Cercospora infection, lesion formation, and toxin-associated virulence; useful antifungal target but direct crop breeding application is indirect because the gene is pathogen-derived
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: possible inhibitory feedback on cercosporin biosynthesis discussed but not proven; NA
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CRP1 is the central experimentally validated gene in the paper. Although homologous to fungal circadian regulators WC-1, circadian function was not directly tested. The paper suggests another blue-light photoreceptor may exist because cercosporin biosynthesis was delayed rather than abolished in mutants.
